primary_diagnosis.name: Acute decompensated heart failure secondary to underlying cardiomyopathy

pathophysiology_summary: An underlying cardiomyopathy has impaired systolic pump function, and the patient has now tipped into acute decompensation. Falling cardiac output triggers neurohormonal activation (renin-angiotensin-aldosterone and sympathetic drive) causing sodium and water retention; rising left-atrial and pulmonary venous pressures drive fluid into the alveolar interstitium (orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, bibasal crackles, hypoxia) while systemic venous congestion produces a raised JVP and bilateral dependent pitting oedema. The rapid ~4 kg weight gain quantifies the volume overload. An S3 gallop and a laterally displaced apex reflect a dilated, volume-loaded, poorly compliant ventricle. Untreated, congestion progresses to frank acute pulmonary oedema and hypoxic respiratory failure. Definitive management is decongestion with a loop diuretic under monitoring of renal function and electrolytes, continuation/optimisation of guideline-directed therapy once stabilised, and identification of the precipitant (ischaemia, arrhythmia, non-adherence, infection).

differential.evidence_basis: Progressive exertional dyspnoea now at rest, orthopnoea, PND and bilateral leg swelling in a patient on cardiac medication — a congestion syndrome.
Leg swelling — but bilateral, symmetrical swelling with systemic congestion argues against a unilateral DVT.
Breathlessness and hypoxia — but the gradual congestive build-up over weeks, not sudden pleuritic onset, makes PE less likely; cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: COPD or lower respiratory tract infection
differential.evidence_basis: Breathlessness with basal crackles — but no fever, no purulent cough and no wheeze, and the systemic congestion points to a cardiac cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage a competent system should already be leading with cardiac decompensation while explicitly keeping PE and an ischaemic precipitant in view.

differential.evidence_basis: Orthopnoea + PND + rapid weight gain + bilateral oedema + resting dyspnoea is a coherent congestion picture; sealed exam (raised JVP, S3, bibasal crackles) confirms it in person.
Ischaemia can precipitate acute decompensation; currently no chest pain, but a silent/atypical ACS trigger cannot be excluded without ECG and troponin.
Absence of wheeze and purulent cough plus systemic venous congestion make a primary airways cause untenable.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen should sharpen toward decongestion + urgent assessment while explicitly retaining an ischaemic-precipitant search.

differential.diagnosis: acute decompensated heart failure secondary to cardiomyopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed congestion syndrome; requires in-person diuresis, bloods, ECG, chest X-ray and echocardiography to confirm structural cause and precipitant.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the structural diagnosis; the correct end-state is urgent in-person assessment, not a remote diagnosis or home management.

red_flags.description: Orthopnoea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea — cannot lie flat, waking gasping
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks significant pulmonary congestion and rising left-heart filling pressures; heralds progression to acute pulmonary oedema.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to appreciate impending pulmonary oedema leads to under-triage and delayed decongestion.

red_flags.description: Breathlessness at rest with reduced oxygen saturation (SpO2 93%)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Resting dyspnoea with hypoxia indicates the patient is at the limit of compensation and needs urgent in-person assessment and oxygen/monitoring capability.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised hypoxia can deteriorate rapidly into hypoxic respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Rapid weight gain (~4 kg in a week) reflecting substantial fluid overload
red_flags.clinical_significance: Quantifies decompensation; an objective, patient-obtainable marker of worsening congestion demanding prompt decongestion.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing accumulation without diuresis worsens pulmonary and systemic congestion.

red_flags.description: Third heart sound (S3) gallop with elevated JVP and bibasal crackles on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective signs of raised filling pressures and pulmonary congestion — confirm decompensated heart failure in person but are not obtainable via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: These confirmatory signs are unavailable remotely, which is precisely why escalation to in-person assessment is mandatory.

red_flags.description: New confusion, syncope or collapse (currently absent) — a marker of critical hypoperfusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: If it appears, signals low-output/hypoperfusion or profound hypoxia and mandates immediate emergency (000) response.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing a change to confusion/collapse would delay a life-saving 000 call.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on bibasal crackles and breathlessness and treating as pneumonia or a COPD exacerbation with antibiotics or a bronchodilator — missing that the driver is cardiac congestion (no fever, no purulent cough, no wheeze; raised JVP and S3).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: DVT/PE anchoring on the leg swelling
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Fixating on swollen legs and pursuing a unilateral DVT/PE pathway — the swelling here is bilateral and symmetrical with systemic congestion, pointing to a cardiac cause, though PE still cannot be fully excluded remotely.

Being falsely reassured by a talking, stable-sounding patient and offering routine GP follow-up or home management — the AI cannot examine the JVP/heart/lungs or start monitored diuresis, so anything short of urgent same-day in-person assessment under-triages a decompensating patient.
Treating the congestion without searching for what tipped the patient over — an ischaemic event (even without chest pain), arrhythmia, or infection needs ECG/troponin and cannot be assessed on chat.
Directing a dose increase of the water tablet over chat without renal-function/electrolyte monitoring or examination — over-diuresis risks acute kidney injury and dangerous electrolyte shifts.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation of a third heart sound (S3) gallop and heart rhythm
Auscultation of bibasal inspiratory crackles / degree of pulmonary congestion
Position and character of the apex beat
Accurate blood pressure, heart rate and respiratory rate without a validated device
True oxygen saturation without a validated home oximeter
12-lead ECG and troponin to identify an ischaemic precipitant
Echocardiogram to confirm structural/cardiomyopathic cause and ejection fraction
Serum renal function and electrolytes needed before/during diuresis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment — emergency department (or, if genuinely unavailable and the patient looks less severe, an urgent same-day acute-care service with a very low threshold to ED) — for monitored diuresis, bloods, ECG, chest X-ray and echocardiography. Advise continuing current cardiac medications, avoiding extra fluids/salt and NSAIDs, and call 000 immediately for severe breathlessness at rest, chest pain, confusion, blue lips or collapse.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the congestion constellation strongly indicates decompensated heart failure, but an ischaemic precipitant, PE and pneumonia cannot be excluded remotely. The dominant risk is UNDER-action: managing at home or deferring to routine follow-up instead of arranging urgent in-person assessment.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHFA/CSANZ Australian Guideline for the Prevention, Detection and Management of Heart Failure 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute decompensation with congestion and hypoxia warrants urgent assessment and loop-diuretic decongestion with monitoring of renal function and electrolytes; identify and treat the precipitant; continue guideline-directed therapy where tolerated.
